heading: Introduction
text: April 27, 2020, Ben Baldwin hit send Tweet announced birth nflfastR, R package designed scrape NFL play--play data allow end-user access speeds quicker similar predecessors (hence name).Thanks work multiple people (@mrcaseB, @benbbaldwin, @TanHo, @LeeSharpeNFL, @thomas_mock … just name ), process getting started advanced analytics using NFL data now easier ever.said, without getting far weeds history behind , -mentioned people responsible shape form current status nflverse, superb collection data R-based packages allows anybody ability access deeply robust NFL data far back 1999 season.nflverse initially birthed Ron Yurko’s nflscrapR project. time development nflscrapR, Ron Ph.D. student Statistics Data Science program Carnegie Mellon University. savvy entire backstory, nflscrapR side project ended creation nflfastR Ben Baldwin Sebastian Carl. Ron officially marked end nflscrapR era beginning nflfastR era tweet September 14, 2020:reply first tweet nflfastR project, Ben explained created original function scrape NFL data creation NFL analytics website. Thankfully, Seb keep ceation relased nflfastR public. “open source” nature R R packages, laundry list companion packages developed alongside nflfastR. original nflfastR package now part larger nflverse packages drive NFL analytics community Twitter beyond.creation nflverse allowed anyboy interested NFL analytics easily access data, manipulate liking, release visiaulizations /metrics wider public thanks large active “analytics community” Twitter beyond. fact, now regular occurence somebody advance R programming ability nflverse go win win Big Data Bowl. 2022 version Big Data Bowl, "30 participants hired work data analytics roles sports, including 22 hired football (“Big Data Bowl: Annual Analytics Contest Explores Statistical Innovations Football,” n.d.). recently, Chargers hired 2020 participate Alex Stern Chiefs hired Marc Richards, member winning 2021 team, Football Research Analyst.Kevin Clark, 2018 article Ringer, explained despite obvious sabermetrics movement baseball, analytics movement NFL “happening front time.” use analytics NFL , however, predate Clark’s article. 2014, Eagles head coach Doug Pederson explained decisons made organization - game planning draft strategy - informed hard data analytics. argument can made, therefore, Ryan Paganetti first analytics-inclined hire brought board Philadelphia build team’s analytics department. 2018, Pederson believed trusted Paganetti’s approach analytics much direct line communication created two games, Paganetti providing head coach math-based recommendations scenario Pederson requested (Awbrey 2020).just five years time since publishing article, become hard ignore analytic movement within NFL. Yet, still much growth happen marriage NFL advanced metrics. example, denying sabermetrics movement drastically “altered baseball’s DNA (Heifetz 2019). Moreover, explained Seth Partnow’s outstanding Midrange Theory: Basketball’s Evolution Age Analytics, analytics movement NBA essentially killed midrange shot (briefly: beneficial try work ball directly basket two shot 3-pointer, additional point worth slightly lower probability 2-point midrange shot) well traditional,”old-school" center position.Compared NBA MLB, NFL playing catchup analytics driving changes equivalent death midrange shot plethroa additional tactics changes baseball sabermetrics. Joe Banner, served President Eagles 2001-2012 Chief Executive Officers Browns 2012-2013, explained hesitation incorporate analytics NFL game planning result game “much driven conventional wisdom extreme degree” (Fortier 2020). Perhaps nobody encapsulates better Pittsburgh Steelers Head Coach Mike Tomlin. asked position analytics 2015 season, Tomlin explained:think ’s play game. can take analytics baseball things like football always going football. got lot respect analytics numbers, ’m going make judgements based numbers. game game. ’s emotional one played emotional driven men. ’s element game can’t measure. Often times decisions weight heavily equation (Kozora 2015).Given Tomlin’s quote 2015, perhaps Steelers pivoted since now analytically inclined. seem case. poll NFL analytics staffers conducted ESPN, Steelers voted one least analytically advanced teams league.large gap least analytically inclined teams (Washington, Tenessee, Cincinnati, New York Giants, Pittsburgh) voted analytically inclined (Baltimore, Cleveland, Philadelphia, Houston). ESPN poll, Browns voted analytics department producing highest level work. One polled spoke fact much outstanding work result General Manager Andrew Berry “true believer,” explaining one “rare guys ’ll come across life think yoursef, ‘Man, guy think different level. Just pure genius.’ ’s one .”article Washington Post, Sam Fortier argues many teams became inspired intimately introduce analytics game planning -field decisions 2017 season. run becoming Super Bowl Champions, Philadelphia Eagles aggressive 4th , going 26 times season converting 17 conversion percentage 65.4%. quick examination visualization data highlights absolutely staggering increase 4th aggressiveness among NFL head coaches 2017-2021:96.3% increase number 4th attempts just 2017 2021. fact, numbers may actually higher quite conservative building plot considering 4th attempts took place offenseive team 5--95% winning probability prior two-minute warning either half. Even conservative limitations, increase staggering. numbers, however, support aggression. week one 2020 2021 season, going 4th “cost teams cumulative 170 percentage points win probability” (Bushnell 2021).Ben Baldwin, using nfl4th package part nflverse, tracked shift NFL coaching mentality regarding 4th decisions comparing 2014’s “go percentage” 2020. results clear:, considering Mike Tomlin’s quote NFL analytics staffers voting Steelers one least analytically driven teams league, surprising Baldwin, using nfl4th package, found Pittsburgh lost win probability either kicking punting “go situations” 2020 NFL season:NFL’s turn towards aggressive 4th-decisions just one many analytics-driven changes occuring league. changes apparent televisions screens Sunday afternoons Fall, others occuring behing scenes (analytics departments working scouting draft preparation, example). Indeed, use analytics NFL tightly engrained see prominent leagues. must remember certainly continued hold outs among NFL coaches (like Mike Tomlin).Despite , “thirst knowledge football excessive sport desire get wins per dollar just high.” pipeline data continues grow, internally league data becomes publically accessible, “smart teams continue leave rock unturned push limits far data can take .” Joe Banner explained NFL long league coaches saying “well, way ’ve always done ,” league ripe major shift (Bechtold 2021).Banner’s belief teams searching every competitive advantage “leave rock unturned” driving force behind book. intents purposes, age analytics NFL stil infancy. seems every new NFL season results amateur analyst bringing groundbreaking model /approach table. chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language
heading: 1.1.1 Getting Weekly Player Stats via load_player_stats()
text: can see ben.weekly output, matched official 2021 regular stats perfectly 3,740 passing yards 605 attempts. code just created several things. First, using nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021) place data R environment DF titled weekly.data. Directly , using another function called load_rosters() populates DF information every player NFL 2021 season (needed moment).Next, group data together alike player_id (every individual player unique ID number). filtering regular season, able summarize weekly data combined statistics summing weekly totals passing yards attempts.Unfortunately, still done. order get Roethlisberger’s name attached , needed , needed complete left_join ben.weekly rosters dataframes. , matching player_id gsis_id within roster information. information combined, able pull just Roethlisberger’s information select columns want.lengthy explanation brings good question: simply filter(player_name == "B.Roethlisberger" & season_type == "REG" rather loading rosters merging two DFs?Truth told, possible Ben Roethliserger. example:, matching official statistics. time much less code without need pull roster information merge .reason work data grouping player_name. excellent example Josh Allen. Let’s attempt “roster, merge” calcuation 2021 total passing yards attempts see happens:output tells us Allen threw 4,049 yards 603 attempts 2021 regular season. check Pro Football Reference page tells us numbers incorrect. fact, 4,407 passing yards 646 attempts. end 358 passing yards 43 attempts short?answer comes Aaron Schatz, creator Football Outsiders, explained Tweet official Buffalo Bills’ scorer, week 3 NFL season, decided refer Allen “Jos.Allen” result Washington Commanders player named “Jonathan Allen.”double check , can run code , group two variables (player_id player_name) see happens:Grouping player_id player_name (well filtering Buffalo), can see , indeed, Josh Allen data twice player_id. Moreover, math, can see numbers two entries add official statistics Pro Football Reference page. verify, can include necessary coding merge Josh’s player_id identical gsis_id roster information:addition merged roster information, able work around issue Josh Allen data twice slightly varied names.
code:
weekly.data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)
rosters <- nflreadr::load_rosters(2021)

ben.weekly <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts)) %>%
  left_join(rosters, by = c("player_id" = "gsis_id")) %>%
  filter(full_name == "Ben Roethlisberger") %>%
  select(player_id, full_name, total.yards, n.attempts)

tibble(ben.weekly)## # A tibble: 1 x 4
##   player_id  full_name          total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>      <chr>                    <dbl>      <int>
## 1 00-0022924 Ben Roethlisberger        3740        605
ben.nomerge <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_name) %>%
  filter(player_name == "B.Roethlisberger" & season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))

tibble(ben.nomerge)## # A tibble: 1 x 3
##   player_name      total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>                  <dbl>      <int>
## 1 B.Roethlisberger        3740        605
josh.allen.nm <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_name) %>%
  filter(player_name == "J.Allen" & season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))

tibble(josh.allen.nm)## # A tibble: 1 x 3
##   player_name total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>             <dbl>      <int>
## 1 J.Allen            4049        603
two.joshies <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & recent_team == "BUF") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'player_id'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
tibble(two.joshies)## # A tibble: 17 x 4
##    player_id  player_name  total.yards n.attempts
##    <chr>      <chr>              <dbl>      <int>
##  1 00-0027685 E.Sanders              0          0
##  2 00-0029000 C.Beasley              0          1
##  3 00-0031588 S.Diggs                0          0
##  4 00-0031787 J.Kumerow              0          0
##  5 00-0033308 M.Breida               0          0
##  6 00-0033466 I.McKenzie             0          0
##  7 00-0033550 D.Webb                 0          0
##  8 00-0033869 M.Trubisky            43          8
##  9 00-0033904 D.Dawkins              0          0
## 10 00-0034857 J.Allen             4049        603
## 11 00-0034857 Jos.Allen            358         43
## 12 00-0035250 D.Singletary           0          0
## 13 00-0035308 T.Sweeney              0          0
## 14 00-0035689 D.Knox                 0          0
## 15 00-0036187 R.Gilliam              0          0
## 16 00-0036196 G.Davis                0          0
## 17 00-0036251 Z.Moss                 0          0
josh.weekly <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts)) %>%
  left_join(rosters, by = c("player_id" = "gsis_id")) %>%
  filter(full_name == "Josh Allen") %>%
  select(player_id, full_name, total.yards, n.attempts)

tibble(josh.weekly)## # A tibble: 1 x 4
##   player_id  full_name  total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>      <chr>            <dbl>      <int>
## 1 00-0034857 Josh Allen        4407        646

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language
heading: 1.1.2 Using load_player_stats() To Find Leaders
text: using load_player_stats() provide ability add context analysis soon see load_pbp(), provide easy efficient way determine weekly season-long leaders many top-level, widely-used NFL statistics. example, determine 2021 leaders air yards per attempt.

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language
heading: 1.1.2.1 An Example: 2021 QB Air Yards per Attempt Leaders
text: example, using group_by combine desired statistics individual quarterback. filtering include just statistics regular season, use summarize function find two items: (1.) total number passing attempts QB outputted new row titled n.attempts regular season total QB’s air yards, outputted new row titled n.airyards.important note final row created summarize function statistic included within load_player_stats(). order find QB’s average air yards per attempt, must use first two items ’ve created simple division (created n.airyards divided n.attempts).Finally, “clear noise” QBs minimal attempts season, included filter include passers least 400 attempts. , arrange new DF sorting QBs descending order average air yards per attempt.end results look like :Russell Wilson, just made cutoff 400 passing attempts, led NFL 2021 9.89 air yards per attempt.
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

ay.per.attempt <- data %>%
  group_by(player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(n.attempts = sum(attempts),
            n.airyards = sum(passing_air_yards),
            ay.attempt = n.airyards / n.attempts) %>%
  filter(n.attempts >= 400) %>%
  arrange(desc(ay.attempt))
tibble(ay.per.attempt)## # A tibble: 24 x 4
##    player_name   n.attempts n.airyards ay.attempt
##    <chr>              <int>      <dbl>      <dbl>
##  1 R.Wilson             400       3955       9.89
##  2 J.Hurts              432       3882       8.99
##  3 B.Mayfield           418       3651       8.73
##  4 M.Stafford           601       5094       8.48
##  5 K.Cousins            561       4575       8.16
##  6 J.Burrow             520       4225       8.12
##  7 D.Carr               626       5084       8.12
##  8 J.Allen              603       4889       8.11
##  9 T.Brady              719       5821       8.10
## 10 T.Bridgewater        426       3424       8.04
## # ... with 14 more rows

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language
heading: 1.2.1 An Example: QB Aggresiveness on 3rd Down
text: Prior writing book, created metric using load_pbp() data coined QB 3rd Aggressiveness. metric designed determine QBs NFL aggressive 3rd situations gauging often throw ball , pass, first line.First, let’s highlight code used create results metric break line--line.can see tibble() output results, Dak Prescott aggressive quarterback 3rd passing situations 2021 season, passing , beyond, line gain just 63% time.creating new dataframe called aggressiveness 2021 play--play originally collected using data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021), use group_by ensure data collected per individual quarterback appending unique passer_id well posteam simply team’s abbreviation (note: posteam variable vital part data collection/manipulation process, play important role taking output data visualization process, covered Chapter 4.However, couple items point clarify code. Moreover, certainly arguments made regarding “capture” scenarios data require “aggressiveness.”using group_by function lump data individual QB, use filter() function. course, want play_types “pass” 3rd downs. However, code, filtering just 3rd situations yards go five ten yards.personal decision end creating metric. logic? less five yards go 3rd , opposing defense able “sell ” pass question offense attempt gain first ground. Conversely, anything ten yards likely results defense selling pass, thus leaving imprint aggressiveness output quarterbacks.sake curiosity, can edit code include passing attempts 3rd 10 yards go first :results quite different first running metric, Dak Prescott now 4th aggressive QB, Kyler Murray moves top approaching nearly 70% aggressiveness rate 3rd . small change highlights important element analytics: much work result coder (ie., ) able justify decision-making process developing filters metric create.case, stand argument including just pass attempts 3rd 5 10 yards go accurate assessment aggressiveness , example, 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation cases.begs question, though: cases 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation?
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021)

aggressiveness <- data %>%
  group_by(passer, passer_id, posteam) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'passer', 'passer_id'. You can override
## using the `.groups` argument.
tibble(aggressiveness)## # A tibble: 30 x 6
##    passer       passer_id  posteam total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>        <chr>      <chr>   <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 D.Prescott   00-0033077 DAL        84         53      0.631
##  2 K.Murray     00-0035228 ARI        60         37      0.617
##  3 J.Hurts      00-0036389 PHI        65         40      0.615
##  4 P.Mahomes    00-0033873 KC         93         56      0.602
##  5 B.Mayfield   00-0034855 CLE        59         35      0.593
##  6 T.Lawrence   00-0036971 JAX        78         46      0.590
##  7 J.Herbert    00-0036355 LAC        87         51      0.586
##  8 D.Jones      00-0035710 NYG        60         35      0.583
##  9 T.Tagovailoa 00-0036212 MIA        60         35      0.583
## 10 M.Stafford   00-0026498 LA         95         54      0.568
## # ... with 20 more rows
aggressiveness.under.10 <- data %>%
  group_by(passer, passer_id, posteam) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'passer', 'passer_id'. You can override
## using the `.groups` argument.
tibble(aggressiveness.under.10)## # A tibble: 33 x 6

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language
heading: 1.2.1.1 QB Aggressiveness: Filtering for “Garbage Time?”
text: initial running QB Aggressiveness metric, Josh Allen 15th aggressive QB NFL 3rd 5 10 yards go. much success Buffalo Bills play 15th place ranking?Bills, conclusion 2021 season, largest positive point differential league 194 (Bills scored 483 points, allowing just 289). Perhaps Allen’s numbers skewed Bills often playing lead late game?account , can add information filter() function attempt remove referenced analytics community “garbage time stats.”Let’s add “garbage time” filter code ’ve already prepared:now using code, included three new items filter(). First, stipulating , aside distance inclusion, want plays occured offense’s win probability 5% 95%, well ensuring plays happen two-minute warning either half.decision range win probability numbers , , personal preference. nflfastR first released, analyst often used 20-80% range win probability. However, Sebastian Carl - one creators nflverse explained package’s Discord:Sebastian Carl: “generally conservative filtering plays using wp. Especially vegas wp model can reach >85% probs early game incorporates market lines. never understood 20% <= wp <= 80%”garbage time" filter. removing ton plays. general advice lower boundary something around 5% (.e., 5% <= wp <= 95%).Ben Baldwin followed Carl’s thoughts:Ben Baldwin: “agree . 20-80% used filter looking run-heavy team (outside range teams change behavior lot). possibly teams behave 4th downs. team player performance.”Based advice, typically stick 5-95% range filtering win probability using play--play data. , case, impact.mentioned, prior filtering garbage time, Allen 15th aggressive QB league nearly 52%. However, filtering garbage time, Allen rose 9th aggressive QB, slight increase percentage 54%.interesting example, though, Dak Prescott Cowboys. Dallas maintained second largest point differntial league (530 points 358 points , 172 point difference). Without garbage time filter, Prescott tops NFL aggressiveness rating 63%.adjusted garbage time? Prescott remained atop NFL aggressiveness rating 65.5%.Allen’s increase standings, Prescott remaining best league, specific metric, possible indicator inclusion “garbage time” filters provides slightly accurate result.
code:
aggressiveness.garbage <- data %>%
  group_by(passer, passer_id, posteam) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10,
         wp > .05, wp < .95, half_seconds_remaining > 120) %>%
  summarize(total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'passer', 'passer_id'. You can override
## using the `.groups` argument.
tibble(aggressiveness.garbage)## # A tibble: 26 x 6
##    passer     passer_id  posteam total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>      <chr>   <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 D.Prescott 00-0033077 DAL        61         40      0.656
##  2 T.Lawrence 00-0036971 JAX        51         33      0.647
##  3 K.Murray   00-0035228 ARI        51         31      0.608
##  4 M.Stafford 00-0026498 LA         79         48      0.608
##  5 P.Mahomes  00-0033873 KC         68         41      0.603
##  6 D.Jones    00-0035710 NYG        50         30      0.6  
##  7 J.Herbert  00-0036355 LAC        67         38      0.567
##  8 M.Jones    00-0036972 NE         57         31      0.544
##  9 J.Allen    00-0034857 BUF        59         32      0.542
## 10 R.Wilson   00-0029263 SEA        56         30      0.536
## # ... with 16 more rows

code:
ay.five.years <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2017:2021)
